questions.question: Define a process in operating system context. How does it differ from a program?
keyPoints: A process is a program in execution with its own execution context
Program is passive (stored on disk), process is active (in memory)
Multiple processes can run same program simultaneously
Process includes: program code, data, stack, PCB, resources

questions.question: Draw and explain the process state diagram with all possible transitions.
keyPoints: Five states: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated
Transitions: New→Ready (admit), Ready→Running (dispatch), Running→Ready (preempt), Running→Waiting (I/O request), Waiting→Ready (I/O complete), Running→Terminated (exit)
Include which entity causes each transition (OS, process, interrupt)

questions.question: What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? List its main components.
keyPoints: PCB is a data structure maintained by kernel for each process
Contains: Process ID, Process State, Program Counter, CPU Registers
Memory Management: Base/Limit registers, Page tables
I/O Status: Open files, I/O devices, pending I/O requests
Scheduling Info: Priority, queue pointers
Accounting: CPU time used, process creation time

questions.question: Explain the concept of context switching with an example.
keyPoints: Context switching is saving/restoring CPU state of processes
Involves: Save PCB of running process, load PCB of new process
Example: Time quantum expires in Round Robin, OS saves current PC/registers, loads next process's PC/registers
Overhead: Pure overhead - no useful work done
Factors: Hardware support, number of registers, speed of memory

questions.question: Differentiate between zombie and orphan processes with examples.
keyPoints: Zombie: Process has terminated but parent hasn't called wait(). Still in process table. Example: Child completes but parent hasn't read exit status.
Orphan: Process whose parent has terminated. Example: Parent process crashes/killed, child continues.
Orphan handling: Adopted by init process (PID 1) in Unix.
Zombie handling: Parent must call wait() to release child entry.

questions.question: What is process scheduling? Discuss the purpose and objectives of process scheduling.
keyPoints: Scheduling decides which process gets CPU when multiple are ready
Objectives: Maximize CPU utilization, maximize throughput
Minimize turnaround time, waiting time, response time
Fairness: All processes get fair CPU time
Balance: Balance between CPU-bound and I/O-bound processes

questions.question: Explain the role of the Dispatcher in process management.
keyPoints: Dispatcher is part of short-term scheduler
Moves process from Ready to Running state
Performs context switch: saves old state, loads new state
Gives CPU control to selected process
Dispatcher latency: time taken for this switching
Should be fast for good system responsiveness

questions.question: With a neat diagram, explain the structure and contents of Process Control Block (PCB). Also explain why PCB is stored in kernel space.
keyPoints: PCB structure diagram showing all fields
Process Identification: PID, Parent PID, User ID
Processor State: PC, Registers, Condition codes, Stack pointer
Process Control: State, Priority, Scheduling info, Resources
Accounting: CPU time, limits, I/O statistics
Memory Management: Page tables, segment tables, base/limit registers
Why kernel space: Protection from user processes, efficient access by kernel, contains sensitive data
Each process has unique PCB in process table

questions.question: Explain the various process states and transitions in a UNIX/Linux system. Also explain how zombie and orphan processes are handled.
keyPoints: Basic states: New, Ready, Running, Sleeping (Waiting), Stopped, Zombie
Running→Sleeping: process waits for I/O or event
Running→Stopped: process receives signal
Parent-child relationship handling
Zombie: Exit status not collected by parent - remains as entry in process table until wait()
Orphan: Parent terminated - adopted by init (PID 1) which calls wait()
Demonstration with fork(), exit(), wait() system calls

questions.question: Discuss the concept of context switching in detail. What is the overhead involved? How can context switching time be minimized?
keyPoints: Definition: Saving and restoring CPU state for process switching
Steps: Save context of current process to its PCB, update PCB, move to appropriate queue, select next process, load its PCB, restore context, jump to saved PC
Overhead: No useful work done, time spent in switch
Factors affecting: Number of registers, memory speed, cache behavior, TLB flush
Minimization: Hardware support, large registers, smart TLB handling, reducing number of switches, optimized data structures for PCB access

questions.question: Explain the three levels of process scheduling in an operating system. Compare short-term, medium-term, and long-term schedulers.
keyPoints: Long-term scheduler: Admits new processes to ready queue, controls degree of multiprogramming, selects from new/terminated to ready queue
Medium-term: Swaps processes in/out of memory, handles suspended states, part of virtual memory management
Short-term (CPU): Selects from ready queue which process to run next, very frequent, must be fast
Comparison: Speed (short fastest), Frequency (short most), Purpose (admission vs memory vs CPU allocation)
